text:
## FAIRWAY DATA EXCHANGE (DX) --- APOC HYPERVISOR 

In this multi-airport configuration, FAIRWAY DATA EXCHANGE (DX) is deployed both locally at each airport and centrally at the CCC. 

## LOCAL FAIRWAY DATA EXCHANGE (DX) 

At airport level, each of the eight airports operates its own FAIRWAY DX instance. 

This local instance collects and organizes the operational data produced by the RESA applications installed on site, such as flight information, passenger processing data, baggage-related information, counters, waiting times, operational events and reference data. 

Once collected, the data is made available locally through a RESA Web API, allowing it to be exposed in a controlled and standardized way without requiring direct access to the operational systems or databases of the airport. 

## CCC FAIRWAY DATA EXCHANGE (DX) 

At CCC level, another FAIRWAY DX instance is installed to act as the central data aggregation point. This central FAIRWAY DX consumes the Web APIs exposed by the eight airports and retrieves the data published by each local FAIRWAY DX. 

The CCC instance therefore does not need to connect individually to every RESA product deployed at each airport; instead, it relies on the standardized API layer provided by the local FAIRWAY DX instances. This approach simplifies architecture, limits the number of external connections to each airport, and provides a clear separation between local airport operations and central supervision services. 

After consuming the airport Web APIs, the CCC FAIRWAY DX consolidates the information received from the eight airports and provides a unified data view at central level. 

This consolidated dataset can then be published to the APOC system. From the APOC perspective, the integration is simplified because APOC connects only once to the CCC, instead of establishing and maintaining separate connections with each individual airport. The CCC therefore becomes the single access point for APOC to retrieve operational information covering all eight airports. 

This architecture provides a scalable and controlled exchange model: each airport remains responsible for collecting and exposing its own local operational data, while the CCC centralizes, harmonizes and republishes the information required by the APOC system. It also reduces integration complexity, improves exchange traceability, and supports a more maintainable supervision architecture for a network of multiple airports. 

Example of description of web api exchange between FAIRWAY DX & APOC HYPERVISOR
